projects.title: Retail KPI Storytelling
projects.impact: On cleaned Online Retail II sales (2009-12-01 to 2011-12-09), the analysis covers 5,881 customers, 36,975 orders, and £17.7M revenue (AOV £480). Champions are 25% of customers but 69% of revenue. Peak month 2010-11 hit £1.2M.
projects.problem: Transaction dumps answer what sold yesterday. They do not answer who to prioritize next month unless you rebuild the data around customers, time, and actionable segments.
projects.summary: Portfolio analysis on the UCI Online Retail II dataset (UK giftware e-commerce, Dec 2009 to Dec 2011). I framed a holiday-priority scenario, cleaned cancellations and missing customer IDs, built RFM segments, and designed YTD/MTD KPI storytelling so a retail lead can decide who deserves retention focus. Method aligned with Formation Data and BI (SQL, Python, RFM, visualization).
projects.tagline: Real Online Retail II data turned into RFM segments and YTD/MTD decisions.
projects.approach: Start from a real trading question, clean the Online Retail II mess, aggregate to customer and time grains, score RFM, label segments, then present YTD/MTD KPIs a stakeholder can scan in a review meeting.
highlights: Grounded the study in UCI Online Retail II, a real UK online retail transaction dataset (CC BY 4.0).
Scenario: who to prioritize before peak giftware season, not a generic chart dump.
Cleaning path for cancellations, missing CustomerID, and returns before RFM.
RFM segments plus YTD/MTD KPI views for recurring business review.
projects.timeline: Formation Data and BI method · portfolio analysis on Online Retail II
projects.roleBody: Owned the analytical narrative: business question, cleaning rules, RFM design, time-based KPIs, and stakeholder framing. Maps to Formation Data and BI craft: business question, cleaning, RFM, and KPI storytelling.
projects.goal: Turn a multi-year retail transaction log into a clear answer to who buys, who is slipping, and how performance moves month to month.

metrics.label: Clean revenue
metrics.detail: Quantity x UnitPrice on cleaned sales lines.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.body:
This case study uses the public Online Retail II dataset from the UCI Machine Learning Repository: real transactions from a UK-based online giftware retailer between December 2009 and December 2011. Many customers are wholesalers. The dataset is CC BY 4.0.

The analytical method matches Formation Data and BI craft (SQL, Python cleaning, RFM, YTD/MTD visualization). MY ROLE, Data analyst framing the business question, cleaning rules, RFM segmentation, and KPI storytelling.

sections.title: Problem
sections.body:
PROBLEM - A raw invoice file tells you units and revenue. It does not tell you which customers deserve retention budget before a seasonal peak.

EXAMPLE - Online Retail II includes cancellations (invoice codes starting with C), missing CustomerID values, and returns mixed into quantity. A naive sum overstates health and breaks customer history.

IMPACT - Teams either trust a polluted total or abandon the file. Neither helps a CRM or ops lead prioritize outreach.

PROBLEM - Without RFM and time windows, every customer looks equally urgent.

EXAMPLE - A high-spend buyer who vanished three months ago sits next to a small but weekly repeat buyer in an unsorted table.

IMPACT - Retention effort spreads thin. Peak season spend hits the wrong people.

sections.title: Goals
sections.body:
BUSINESS GOALS - Support a monthly trading or CRM review with a short list of customer priorities and a clean read on YTD/MTD performance.

USER GOALS - Give an analyst a repeatable path from raw UCI extract to segments and KPI views.

Give a decision-maker a scannable answer to who is Champions, who is At Risk, and how this month compares to the year so far.

sections.title: Impact
sections.body:
Computed on the public Online Retail II extract after the cleaning rules in this study.

Clean window: 2009-12-01 to 2011-12-09. Raw rows 1,067,371; missing CustomerID 243,007; cancellations among identified customers 18,744; cleaned sales lines 805,620.

Trading snapshot: 5,881 customers, 36,975 orders, £17.7M revenue, AOV £480. Peak month 2010-11 at £1.2M. YTD 2011 £8.3M; MTD 2011-12 £518k.

sections.title: Stakeholders
sections.body:
Primary reader: a retail ops or CRM lead preparing for peak giftware season.

JOB TO BE DONE - When holiday demand rises, I want a clear shortlist of customers to retain or reactivate so outreach budget follows value and risk, not gut feel.

Secondary reader: the analyst who must defend cleaning rules and segment definitions in that same meeting.

sections.title: Scenario: holiday priority
sections.body:
DATA QUESTION - Who should we prioritize before the next giftware peak: protect Champions, nurture Loyal customers, or reactivate At Risk spenders?

The storyboard that follows is the working session: clean the file, rebuild customer grain, score RFM, read YTD/MTD, then leave the room with a decision mix rather than a pile of charts with no owner.

sections.title: Process: clean the real mess
sections.body:
Online Retail II is useful because it is messy in the ways production retail data is messy.

Cleaning rules: drop rows without CustomerID for customer analytics, treat invoices starting with C as cancellations, compute line revenue as Quantity times UnitPrice, and keep returns visible when they matter for quality checks.

INSIGHT - If you skip this step, RFM and YTD totals inherit fiction.

images.alt: Cleaning funnel from raw Online Retail II rows to positive sales lines
images.caption: Funnel: 1,067,371 raw rows down to 805,620 positive sales lines with CustomerID.

sections.title: Process: SQL and customer grain
sections.body:
After cleaning, the analysis moves to invoice and customer grains: joins and aggregations that answer revenue, order counts, and AOV over time.

Formation craft shows up here: CTEs, window functions, and views that make YTD/MTD reusable instead of one-off notebook cells.

images.caption: Country mix after cleaning: UK concentration with international wholesale tails.

sections.title: Process: RFM segmentation
sections.body:
Per customer:

Recency - days since last purchase.

Frequency - count of invoices.

Monetary - sum of revenue.

Scores become labeled segments such as Champions, Loyal, Potential, At Risk, and Lost. Labels matter more than cluster IDs in a business review because they imply action.

This approach follows the retail RFM tradition associated with Online Retail analyses (including Chen et al. on RFM for online retail), adapted for a clear stakeholder narrative.

images.caption: Champions 1,482 · Loyal 1,222 · At Risk 824.
Champions hold 69% of cleaned revenue with 25% of customers.

sections.title: Process: YTD/MTD storytelling
sections.body:
Time KPIs answer the other half of the briefing: how are we doing this month versus the year so far.

YTD and MTD views sit beside RFM so segment decisions are grounded in current trading pace, not only lifetime value.

images.alt: Monthly cleaned revenue over Online Retail II period
images.caption: Monthly pulse with peak in 2010-11 at £1.2M.

sections.title: Decision storyboard
sections.body:
DECISION - Protect Champions (1,482 customers, 69% of revenue) with white-glove retention, keep Loyal (1,222) in a steady nurture track, and run a focused reactivation offer for At Risk (824 customers, 9% of revenue) who used to spend.

INSIGHT - The point of RFM is not prettier colors. It is a shorter meeting: fewer customers discussed, clearer owners, and a mix you can defend with recency and spend evidence.

DATA QUESTION - After the campaign window, which At Risk customers returned, and did Champions hold their share of YTD revenue?

images.alt: Revenue concentration by RFM segment for decision mix
images.caption: Decision mix follows revenue concentration, not equal customer counts.

sections.title: What we learned
sections.body:
Real public data beats a synthetic shop if you are honest about source, license, and what you still need to compute.

Cleaning rules are product decisions. They belong in the case study, not a hidden cell.

Segments without actions are still a chart dump. Champions and At Risk only earn their place when the decision chapter names what happens next.

sections.title: Future
sections.body: Charts and cleaned KPIs are now attached from the Online Retail II run. Next depth: cohort retention and country-mix cuts still tied to the same holiday-priority question, plus any Formation SQL artifacts you want shown as process proof.

projects.nextSteps: Optional depth: cohort retention and UK vs international wholesaler cuts, still framed by the holiday-priority decision.

projects.title: Trusted Test Data Reporting
projects.impact: Moved reporting from ad-hoc manual collation to a repeatable JIRA-backed workflow with clearer anomaly visibility and faster turnaround for cross-functional reviews.
projects.problem: Cross-functional engineering teams needed faster, trustworthy answers from high-volume test and diagnostic data - manual collation slowed decisions and hid anomalies.
projects.summary: At Capgemini Engineering and KPIT Engineering, I analyzed and validated large Hardware-in-the-Loop and system test datasets, automated collection workflows in JIRA, and delivered clear reports for hardware, software, and systems teams.
projects.tagline: Large validation datasets cleaned, tracked, and turned into decisions.
projects.approach: Validated large-scale HiL and system test datasets, automated data collection and defect tracking in JIRA, and wrote Python scripts to generate result data and reporting for hardware, software, and systems teams.
highlights: Validated large-scale Hardware-in-the-Loop and system test datasets, surfacing performance trends and anomalies for engineering teams.
Automated data collection and defect-tracking workflows in JIRA to improve traceability and cut analysis turnaround.
Built Python scripts to generate and analyze result data, improving reporting accuracy for hardware, software, and systems teams.
projects.roleBody: Analyzed and validated large Hardware-in-the-Loop and system test datasets, automated collection and defect tracking, and built clearer anomaly visibility for engineering teams.
projects.goal: Give cross-functional engineering teams faster, trustworthy answers from high-volume test and diagnostic data.

projects.title: Chetek Sports | Tennis Referee Companion
projects.impact: A build-ready UX package: research artifacts, a sharp persona, process wireframes, and a high-fidelity French system spanning onboarding, licence trust gates, planning and assignments, live scoring, rules learning with a quiz, and post-match reporting. The handoff let engineering build from decisions, not guesses.
projects.problem: Mid-point, an arbiter has seconds to apply the right rule. Instead they juggle bulky physical rulebooks, static ITF/ATP/WTA PDFs, and thin third-party summaries, none of them built for a phone held courtside. Every extra tap or page flip is hesitation in front of players, and hesitation reads as doubt.
projects.summary: Vivez votre match comme les pros. Chetek Sports is a native iOS companion I designed for French tennis arbiters, the people running the match from the chair. I took it from lived friction and research through persona, wireframes, and a high-fidelity French system that covers the whole match day: onboarding, licence trust, assignments, live scoring, rules learning, and post-match reporting. Research and hi-fi by me; development by Ilyes.
projects.tagline: UX for tennis arbiters: research, French flows, and a full match-day system for the chair.
projects.approach: I started from the client arbiter's real mid-match friction rather than a sports-app template. I mapped the competitive gap, ran a survey on lookup speed and offline need, and shaped persona Walid around one primary seat. From there I moved through low-fi wireframes and mid-fi court scoring, then into a broader hi-fi platform so the product owned the full match day, not just the score.
highlights: Grounded the product in a working arbiter's mid-match friction, not a generic sports brief.
Diagnosed why official rulebooks, PDFs, and thin apps all fail under live time pressure.
Persona Walid Chetek: a Marseille arbiter-coach who needs speed, offline access, and clear interpretation.
Carried the story from wireframes to mid-fi court scoring, then a full hi-fi system for the official seat.
Kept one calm French voice across auth, planning, scoring, and rules so the product felt official.
projects.roleBody: I owned research and product UX end to end for a freelance brief: competitive scan, survey synthesis, persona, information architecture, user flows, and high-fidelity French UI. The brief came from a tennis arbiter who lived the problem on court, so every decision could be checked against a real user. Ilyes handled development from my handoff.
projects.goal: Give French-speaking tennis arbiters a companion they trust mid-match: faster orientation under pressure, offline-minded thinking, and match tools from tirage au sort through live scoring, all in one official voice.

metrics.label: Design arc
metrics.detail: Dec 2022 to Jul 2023, research through hi-fi handoff.

metrics.label: Primary user
metrics.detail: Every screen serves the arbiter on court, not spectators.

metrics.label: French UI
metrics.detail: Microcopy in one official voice across the whole match day.

metrics.label: Match-day span
metrics.detail: Onboarding, trust, planning, scoring, rules, reporting.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.body:
Chetek Sports is a native iOS companion for tennis arbiters in France. The brief came from a working arbiter who was still juggling rulebooks, PDFs, and thin tools while the point was live, so the product had a real user from day one.

The ambition was not another scoreboard. It was a calm, official-feeling companion that owns the whole match day, from the moment an arbiter opens the app to the report they file after the last point.

MY ROLE - I owned research and product UX end to end: competitive scan, survey synthesis, persona, information architecture, flows, and high-fidelity French UI. Ilyes handled development from my handoff.

sections.title: Problem
sections.body:
PROBLEM - When a call is contested, the arbiter needs the right rule now, in front of players, not after flipping through three sources.

EXAMPLE - Physical rulebooks are bulky and slow. Official PDFs are complete but awkward to search mid-point. Switching between a book and phone tabs breaks eye contact and rhythm exactly when composure matters most.

IMPACT - Slow, uncertain lookups cost the arbiter authority. Errors creep in, players push harder, and the match loses its rhythm.

PROBLEM - No dedicated tennis-arbiter companion combined official depth with real mobile speed.

EXAMPLE - ITF, ATP, and WTA materials are authoritative but static. Third-party summaries are thin and unofficial. General officiating apps rarely handle tennis edge cases like tie-breaks, ball changes, or side switches.

IMPACT - That gap was the opening: official seriousness with phone speed, designed specifically for the chair.

images.alt: Research slide on why the arbiter companion exists
images.caption: I opened with the why, framed from lived courtside friction rather than a feature wishlist.

images.alt: Market research slide with claim and problem
images.caption: Market view: arbiters need speed and clarity under live pressure, and nothing served that well.

images.alt: Competitive analysis of rulebooks and officiating apps
images.caption: Competitive scan: existing tools offered depth without speed, or speed without real tennis depth.

sections.title: Goals
sections.body:
BUSINESS GOALS - Deliver a companion arbiters trust mid-match, and hand engineering a sharp, build-ready package from research through hi-fi.

Move beyond a static rule PDF to owning match-day control: orientation, setup, scoring, and learning in one coherent product voice.

USER GOALS - Faster orientation under pressure, offline-minded thinking for dead venue connectivity, and tools from tirage au sort through live scoring, with no fan clutter competing for attention.

A calm French interface that feels official on the chair instead of like a generic sports template.

sections.title: Impact
sections.body:
This was a freelance arc delivered in phases: research, low-fi wireframes, mid-fi scoring exploration, then a fuller hi-fi system for the official seat. The measures below are honest scope markers for a solo research-to-hi-fi brief, not invented adoption or conversion numbers.

The real outcome is a package an engineer could build from with confidence: every screen ties back to a research input, a persona need, or an explicit match-day job.

sections.title: Our Users
sections.body:
Walid Chetek is 42 and based in Marseille: five years as an arbiter, ten as a coach. He is comfortable on a phone yet still stuck with bulky rulebooks when the point is hot.

JOB TO BE DONE - When a rule is contested mid-match, I want a calm, searchable companion so I can decide quickly without breaking the rhythm of the court.

He asked for offline access, quick reference, scenario learning, and even a digital coin toss. Anchoring to one real seat shaped the product far more than any generic sports template, and it gave me a clear test for every later feature: does this help Walid decide faster on court?

images.alt: Persona card for Walid Chetek tennis arbiter
images.caption: Persona first: one primary seat, one clear job, a real test for every feature that followed.

sections.title: Process: Sketches
sections.body:
I mapped the full match day in low-fidelity wireframes before touching visual design: login, competition pick, the rencontres schedule, team lineups, match-code entry, tirage au sort, match parameters, the pre-match recap, and the first court-scoring ideas.

At this stage I cared about structure and French microcopy jobs, not polish. Seeing the whole flow in grey boxes is where I cut taps between ready and live, and where the coin-toss and side-selection logic got pinned down.

images.caption: Login and entry, kept to one job.
Choose a competition or drop into a match code.
Rencontres: the day as a scannable schedule.
Team lineups from singles through doubles.
Quick match-code path for one-off matches.
Tirage au sort: toss, serve, and side in one place.
Match parameters: format, ball change, tie-break rules.
Pre-match recap to confirm before the first point.
An early edge case: the tie-break points modal.
First court-scoring idea, tap targets over chrome.

sections.title: Process: Early designs
sections.body:
Mid-fidelity screens tightened the auth path and proved the core bet: scoring as muscle memory. A court you can tap, with Ace, Filet, Faute, and F. de pied within reach while the point is still alive.

This is where the LiveScore companion stopped being a diagram and started to feel real, with the active side flipping as play switches ends. Testing it here, before hi-fi, kept the later platform expansion grounded in a scoring model that already worked.

images.caption: Welcome, setting the calm entry tone.
Login without friction before a match.
Register, kept short.
Forgot password, one clear recovery start.
Verification step in the same voice.
Success state that closes the recovery loop.
Light scoring theme for bright courts.
The core bet: a tappable court, actions in reach.
A live scoring state mid-game.
Active side flips as players switch ends.

sections.title: Process: Listening
sections.body:
Alongside the client arbiter, I drew on the difficulties officials share when applying rules live. The survey pressed on current resources, lookup speed, unclear interpretations, must-have features, and how critical offline access is when venue connectivity dies.

That input did not sit in a slide. It set clear priorities: calm auth, schedule orientation, scoring as muscle memory, and, later, rules learning that lives beside the official documents.

images.alt: User survey questions for tennis arbiters
images.caption: Survey prompts that ranked speed, clarity, and offline need over nice-to-haves.

sections.title: Looks new on the chair
sections.body:
The final system opens with court light and a clear promise for the people running the match. The splash and onboarding welcome set an official, confident voice before any form asks for attention.

First impressions carry trust here: an arbiter should feel this app belongs on the chair with them.

images.caption: Splash: brand and promise in one calm frame.
Onboarding welcome that speaks to the arbiter, not a fan.

sections.title: Onboarding the job
sections.body:
Onboarding teaches the job in short, single-purpose beats: planning, scoring, availability, then a setup-complete confirmation. Each screen owns one idea so the arbiter learns what the product does before match day, not during it.

Keeping onboarding this disciplined mirrored a core principle from the whole system: one screen, one job.

images.caption: Planning: see the day at a glance.
Scoring: the core action, previewed.
Availability: tell the system when you can officiate.
Setup complete: ready for match day.

sections.title: Auth that stays calm
sections.body:
Auth had to be boring in the best way. Login, register, and verification stay in one calm French voice so arbiters never fight forms before a match.

I treated recovery and verification as a single considered path rather than a pile of disconnected screens, so the tone stays official even when something goes wrong.

images.caption: Login: fast, familiar, low friction.
Sign up: only what the role needs.
Verification in the same official voice.

sections.title: Trust before tools
sections.body:
For an officiating product, credibility is the feature. Home can stay read-only until the licence is verified, so the app never lets an unverified account act as an official.

The pending state and verification path make that trust explicit. Blocked actions are shown as pending, not hidden behind buttons that quietly fail, which respects the arbiter and the integrity of the match.

images.caption: Home in a pending state: honest about what is not unlocked yet.
Licence verification: the trust gate before real tools open.

sections.title: The day is assignments
sections.body:
Tournament days are logistics before they are points. This path keeps the arbiter oriented: calendar view, full assignment detail, venue and role notes, and a pre-match readiness screen.

Pulling all of that into one flow means an arbiter can arrive prepared, knowing the court, the role, and the context, before the first serve.

images.caption: Planning calendar: where the day starts.
Assignment detail: match, role, and venue in one place.
Pre-match readiness before walking to the chair.

sections.title: Live on court
sections.body:
Live scoring is the loudest idea in the product, and the one I validated earliest. Large point actions, a running log of recent events, and a match summary that closes the loop after the final point.

The layout puts the court and its tap targets first and chrome second, so scoring stays muscle memory even under pressure. It is the direct descendant of the mid-fi court I tested in the process phase.

images.caption: Live scoring: court and actions first, chrome second.
Match summary that closes the loop after the last point.

sections.title: Rules in the pocket
sections.body:
Rules become searchable in the pocket, with recent updates surfaced and a quiz path for scenario learning. Instead of living in a separate PDF tab, interpretation sits right beside the official documents.

This is where the product answers the original problem head on: the right rule, and the reasoning behind it, available in seconds without breaking the arbiter's focus.

images.caption: Reglements: official documents, searchable on the phone.
Quiz question: scenario learning, not passive reading.
Quiz results that reinforce the tricky interpretations.

sections.title: After the match
sections.body:
The match is not done at the last point. Post-match reporting closes the day, and a clear confirmation makes the handoff to organizers feel finished and official.

Treating reporting as part of the core loop, rather than an afterthought, keeps the product honest to the arbiter's real responsibilities.

images.caption: Report confirmation: the day handed off cleanly.

sections.title: Support when stuck
sections.body:
When the product or the rules still leave a question, help and live chat stay within reach. For an official tool, reachable support is itself part of trust on match day.

It is the safety net behind the confidence the rest of the system projects.

images.caption: Help center for the common questions.
Live chat for the moments that need a person.

sections.title: What we learned
sections.body:
Designing for one primary seat clarified everything. Every time a spectator or generic-sports feature tempted its way in, the persona test cut it: it did not help Walid decide faster on court.

French microcopy was not decoration, it was trust. Auth, planning, scoring, and rules all had to sound like the same official voice, or the product would feel unofficial.

Process artifacts earned their place. Validating the tappable court in mid-fi is what made the later platform expansion honest instead of theatrical, because the riskiest interaction was already proven.

sections.title: Future
sections.body:
After handoff, the most honest next step is arbiter testing in live tournaments: how deep offline rule lookup really needs to go, whether scenario learning changes on-court confidence, and which assignment and scoring edge cases only surface when the point is hot.

Any post-launch iteration should follow real match-day use and measured behaviour, not more screens added for their own sake.

projects.nextSteps: Test with arbiters in live tournaments: offline rule-lookup depth, scenario learning's effect on confidence, and the scoring and assignment edge cases that only appear when the point is hot. Let real match-day behaviour, not screen count, drive the next iteration.

projects.title: BryTek | Online Learning Platform
projects.impact: A complete UX package for a three-sided EdTech product, delivered beside a learning-analytics case so the client could approve scope with both product shape and retention evidence in hand.
projects.problem: Online learning platforms often split the story: a pretty marketing site for discovery, then a disconnected instructor console for teaching and money. Learners, teachers, and owners need one coherent BryTek voice from first visit to the dashboard.
projects.summary: BryTek is an online learning platform I designed as one half of a client justification package: dark marketing pages for courses, events, and blogs, a role gate for Admin, Teacher, and Student, and a full instructor suite for dashboard KPIs, courses, earnings, messages, and settings. I also owned the paired data study so the client could see both the product experience and the retention logic behind the instructor cockpit before committing to build.
projects.tagline: EdTech UX for BryTek, paired with a data study to justify the product to the client.
projects.approach: I treated BryTek as a two-part case for the client: design proves how the product feels from discovery to the instructor cockpit; the companion learning-analytics study proves why that cockpit must surface early learner risk, not only enrollments and earnings. On the design side, public discovery and logged-in teaching share one visual system, with role-aware entry first.
highlights: Built design and data together so the client could justify investing in BryTek with both lenses.
Mapped a three-role entry model (Admin/Owner, Teacher, Student) before auth forms.
Designed public discovery plus an instructor cockpit for KPIs, courses, earnings, and messages.
Kept one dark visual system across marketing and logged-in tools.
projects.timeline: Client justification study: UX + paired learning-analytics case
projects.role: Product / UI designer (paired with data analysis)
projects.roleBody: I owned the visual and interaction design for BryTek and worked the paired data study so the client had a complete justification package: information architecture across public and instructor surfaces, role-based entry, auth modals, and the instructor dashboard suite, plus evidence that early learner silence is a real retention risk the product should surface.
projects.goal: Give the client a clear case for BryTek: a coherent product experience learners and teachers can trust, and a data-backed reason the instructor cockpit must support early outreach.

metrics.label: Entry model
metrics.detail: Admin/Owner, Teacher, and Student from the first gate.

metrics.label: Product split
metrics.detail: Public marketing site plus logged-in instructor workspace.

metrics.label: Instructor suite
metrics.detail: Dashboard, courses, course detail, earnings, messages, settings.

metrics.label: Visual voice
metrics.detail: Dark charcoal base with teal actions and orange accents.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.body:
BryTek is an online learning platform designed as a dark, modern EdTech experience. The product has to sell learning in public and run teaching in private: courses, events, and blogs on the outside; KPIs, course management, and earnings on the inside.

I worked this design case together with the BryTek learning-analytics study. The point was not a decorative portfolio pair. It was to justify the work to the client: show what BryTek looks and feels like, and show why the instructor cockpit needs early retention intelligence before build budget is locked.

MY ROLE - Product / UI designer for the marketing site, role gate, auth flows, and instructor suite, working in parallel with the data study that backs the teacher dashboard.

sections.title: Problem
sections.body:
PROBLEM - Prospective learners meet a marketing site, while instructors live in a console. If those surfaces feel like different products, trust drops before the first lesson.

EXAMPLE - A visitor finds a course on the public grid, then hits a login path that never asks whether they are a student or a teacher. The instructor later opens a flat list of courses with no earnings or rating context.

IMPACT - Learners bounce. Teachers cannot see whether a course is healthy. The platform looks unfinished even when the screens exist.

PROBLEM - Instructor work is multi-signal: enrollments, ratings, revenue, messages. Spreading those signals across disconnected pages slows every morning check-in.

EXAMPLE - Revenue lives on one page, ratings on another, and recent comments nowhere near the course list.

IMPACT - Teachers manage by inbox and guesswork instead of a single cockpit.

sections.title: Goals
sections.body:
BUSINESS GOALS - Give the client a credible BryTek brand and product shape they can approve, then convert visitors into the right account type without confusion.

Prove, beside the data study, that the instructor workspace is worth building because course health and earnings sit next to a real retention job.

USER GOALS - Learners: find courses, events, and articles quickly, then sign in or sign up as a student.

Teachers: land on a dashboard that answers what happened overnight before diving into course detail, earnings, or messages.

sections.title: Impact
sections.body:
This case measures design scope, not invented platform revenue. The instructor dashboard shows sample KPI figures for UI realism; those numbers are not claimed as live business results.

The outcome for the client is a justification package: a coherent UX system ready to hand to engineering, paired with the learning-analytics case that explains what the cockpit should prioritize when learners go silent.

sections.title: Our Users
sections.body:
BryTek starts with three seats at the role gate.

Admin / Owner - Needs oversight and platform control. Entry must feel official, not like a student signup.

Teacher - Needs courses, ratings, earnings, and messages in one daily loop.

JOB TO BE DONE - When I open BryTek in the morning, I want a clear view of enrollments, ratings, and revenue so I know which course needs attention today.

Student - Needs a simple path into learning after discovering courses on the public site.

images.alt: BryTek role selection modal for Admin, Teacher, and Student
images.caption: Role gate: choose the seat before the form.

sections.title: Process: Two surfaces, one brand
sections.body:
I treated BryTek as one information architecture with two modes.

Public mode sells the platform: About, Courses, Events, Blogs, Contact. Logged-in instructor mode runs the business of teaching: Dashboard, My Courses, Earnings, Messages, Settings.

The join point is the role gate and auth modals. Get that wrong and the rest of the UI cannot recover trust.

sections.title: Final: Public discovery
sections.body: The public site is a dark editorial layout: strong hero, search, featured cards, and newsletter capture. Courses, events, and blogs reuse the same rhythm so discovery feels familiar across content types.

images.caption: About: brand story on the public surface.
Courses: featured cards plus a dense list.
Events: time-bound discovery.
Blogs: editorial depth beside courses.
Contact: low-friction outreach.

sections.title: Final: Getting in
sections.body:
Entry is explicit. Visitors pick Admin, Teacher, or Student, then move into trainer login or trainee sign-in / sign-up modals with Google and email paths.

One polish note for validation: a modal headline spells BryTeck while the logo reads BryTek. Align the spelling before handoff.

images.caption: Tell us who you are first.
Trainer path: welcome carousel plus form.
Trainee path: sign in with the same dark system.

sections.title: Final: Instructor cockpit
sections.body: The instructor dashboard is the product thesis: KPI cards first, then ratings, course overview, recent activity, and revenue. Teachers should feel the health of their catalog before opening a single course.

images.alt: BryTek instructor dashboard with KPI cards and charts
images.caption: Dashboard: enrollments, ratings, revenue, and activity in one morning view.
Learner-side depth: the other half of the marketplace.

sections.title: Final: Courses and earnings
sections.body: My Courses and course detail keep teaching work concrete. Earnings closes the loop for instructors who need money and performance in the same product voice as the catalog.

images.caption: My Courses: catalog management without leaving the cockpit.
Course detail: depth when a title needs attention.
Earnings: revenue as a first-class instructor job.

sections.title: Final: Stay in the loop
sections.body: Messages and settings keep the instructor suite complete. Communication and account control sit in the same navigation as teaching and money, not as orphan pages.

images.caption: Messages: comments and conversations next to teaching work.
Settings: account control in the same system.

sections.title: What we learned
sections.body:
Design alone is a mood board. Design plus a data study is an argument a client can fund.

Role before form reduces auth confusion in multi-sided products. Asking Admin, Teacher, or Student first is cheaper than recovering from a wrong account type later.

Instructor dashboards should lead with decisions, not chrome. KPI cards and ratings belong above course lists, and the paired data case shows which decision (early outreach) matters most.

Public and private surfaces must share one visual voice, or EdTech products feel like a brochure stapled to an admin tool.

sections.title: Future
sections.body:
Keep the client brief explicit: timeline, ownership, and whether Admin flows need dedicated screens beyond the role gate.

Next design depth: learner course-taking flows after trainee sign-in, empty states for new instructors, and a pass to align BryTek spelling across modals.

Usability testing with one teacher and one student would pressure-test the morning dashboard and the discovery-to-signup path, while the data case stays the evidence spine for retention.

projects.nextSteps: Keep design and data paired in client reviews, align BryTek spelling, and deepen learner post-login flows after the trainee gate.

projects.title: BryTek | Learning Analytics for Instructor Decisions
projects.impact: On the Open University Learning Analytics Dataset (32,593 enrollments across 7 modules), 53% end Fail or Withdrawn. Silent starters (14% with zero VLE clicks in the first 14 days) reach 91% at-risk. Lowest early-engagement quintile: 86% at-risk vs 28% in the highest.
projects.problem: In online teaching, learners can disappear quietly. A BryTek instructor who only sees enrollments and earnings cannot tell who has gone silent early enough to help, so support arrives after fail or withdrawal instead of before. A client cannot approve that cockpit on UI fiction alone.
projects.summary: I worked the BryTek data study together with the BryTek UX case to justify the product to the client: design shows how the platform feels, and this analysis shows why the instructor cockpit must surface early retention risk. Distance learning creates a human gap: teachers cannot see the room, so early Virtual Learning Environment (VLE) silence often warns that a learner is drifting toward fail or withdrawal. I ground that job in the public Open University Learning Analytics Dataset (CC BY 4.0), published for learning-analytics research (Kuzilek, Hlosta, Zdrahal, Nature Scientific Data, 2017). Public Open University data is used as a scientific stand-in, not BryTek production telemetry.
projects.tagline: Retention evidence for BryTek, paired with UX, to justify the product to the client.
projects.approach: Treat design and data as one client justification package. Start from the human retention problem in online teaching, place it in the Open University learning-analytics tradition (early at-risk detection from VLE behaviour), then measure outcomes and early clicks on the Open University Learning Analytics Dataset so the BryTek instructor queue has a defensible basis the client can fund.
highlights: Paired with BryTek UX so the client sees product shape and retention evidence in one case.
Human stake: online teachers cannot see the room; early silence warns before fail or withdrawal.
Scientific stake: Open University learning-analytics research on early at-risk detection from VLE behaviour.
Uses the Open University Learning Analytics Dataset (Nature Scientific Data / UCI, CC BY 4.0) as public evidence.
projects.timeline: Client justification study: data + paired BryTek UX · Open University Learning Analytics Dataset (2013 - 2014)
projects.platform: Learning analytics (Python / pandas / EdTech decision framing)
projects.roleBody: Owned the analytical half of the client justification package: why the study exists (human retention + scientific tradition), source honesty, early-engagement risk, module health, and an intervention queue a BryTek teacher could act on, delivered beside the UX case so the client could approve both product and evidence.
projects.goal: Help the client say yes to BryTek with evidence: prove the instructor cockpit should surface who is going silent early, using a public learning-analytics dataset published for this class of retention questions.

metrics.label: Silent starters
metrics.detail: At-risk among the 14% with zero clicks in the first 14 days.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.body:
I worked this data study together with the BryTek UX case to justify the product to the client. Design alone shows screens. Design plus retention evidence shows why those screens deserve budget.

This study exists because online teaching hides the human signal that a classroom makes obvious. On BryTek, a teacher can see enrollments, ratings, and earnings. What they still need is an early answer to a quieter question: who has stopped showing up in the learning environment before the first failed assessment or a withdrawal letter.

The UX case designed that instructor cockpit. This data case fills it with a retention logic learning-analytics research already treats as serious work, so the client can approve BryTek with both product shape and evidence in hand.

MY ROLE - Data analyst on the client justification package: human and scientific context, then early engagement risk on a public Open University dataset, paired with UX.

sections.title: Why this study: human and scientific context
sections.body:
CLIENT CONTEXT - The brief needed more than a pretty instructor dashboard. I owned both the BryTek design study and this data study so the client could decide with a full case: what the product looks like, and why early learner outreach belongs in scope. Approving UX without retention evidence would leave the cockpit half-justified.

HUMAN CONTEXT - In distance and online learning, absence is easy to miss. A learner can register, open nothing, and drift toward fail or withdrawal without a corridor conversation. Tutors and support teams only intervene if the product surfaces risk while there is still time. That is the morning job behind BryTek's teacher dashboard: not prettier KPIs, but a shortlist of people who need a human message this week.

SCIENTIFIC CONTEXT - Learning analytics exists to use learner data to improve the learning experience and guide support. At the Open University, that line of work became operational in systems such as OU Analyse, built for early identification of students at risk of failing so tutors and Student Support Teams can act. The Open University Learning Analytics Dataset was published so researchers and practitioners can study those questions with real, anonymized course, demographic, assessment, and Virtual Learning Environment (VLE) interaction data from 2013 and 2014 presentations (Kuzilek, Hlosta, Zdrahal, Nature Scientific Data 4:170171, 2017; CC BY 4.0; also available via UCI and OU Analyse).

WHY THIS DATASET HERE - BryTek does not ship production LMS logs in a client pitch. The Open University Learning Analytics Dataset is the public evidence base that matches the same job: early risk from learning behaviour, not invented grades. This analysis does not claim to reproduce OU Analyse as a product. It borrows the scientific question (who is at risk early?) and turns it into a BryTek instructor decision story the client can fund beside the UX.

sections.title: Problem
sections.body:
PROBLEM - Online teachers lose the room. Without an engagement risk view, BryTek's morning cockpit cannot tell who needs outreach today.

EXAMPLE - Two courses can show similar enrollment counts while one is filled with learners who never opened the VLE in the first two weeks.

IMPACT - Support waits until after a failed assessment or a withdrawal. The human cost is a learner who might have stayed with one timely message; the product cost is a dashboard that looks busy and still fails the teacher.

PROBLEM - Retention research needs clean outcome and time definitions. Raw LMS tables mix Pass, Distinction, Fail, Withdrawn, and daily click summaries until you choose what 'at risk' means and how early you look.

EXAMPLE - The Open University Learning Analytics Dataset labels final results and stores VLE click summaries on a relative course calendar. Those fields only become actionable when you define an early window and an intervention outcome.

IMPACT - Without those definitions, 'engagement' stays a decorative chart instead of a queue a teacher can defend.

sections.title: Goals
sections.body:
BUSINESS GOALS - Give the client a clear case for BryTek: retention evidence that stands beside the UX package, so approving the instructor product is not a leap of faith.

Show that BryTek's instructor cockpit answers a real retention problem learning-analytics research already takes seriously.

USER GOALS - Give a BryTek teacher a shortlist: silent starters first, then low early engagement, with module context so staffing follows course risk.

Give the client the human reason (protect drifting learners) and the scientific reason (Open University early at-risk tradition + published dataset) in one story they can verify before build.

sections.title: Impact
sections.body:
Computed on the public Open University Learning Analytics Dataset after joining studentInfo to early VLE activity (days 0 to 14).

Scale: 32,593 enrollments, 28,785 students, 7 modules, 22 presentations.

Outcomes: Distinction 3,024, Pass 12,361, Fail 7,052, Withdrawn 10,156. Combined Fail + Withdrawn: 53%.

Silent starters (14% with zero early clicks): 91% at-risk. Lowest early-engagement quintile 86% at-risk vs 28% in the highest. Highest-risk module in this cut: CCC (62% at-risk, 45% withdrawn).

Those numbers matter because they quantify the human gap: a large share of enrollments end badly, and the worst outcomes concentrate where early VLE activity never starts.

sections.title: Stakeholders
sections.body:
Primary reader: a BryTek Teacher using the instructor cockpit from the UX case.

JOB TO BE DONE - When I open BryTek in the morning, I want a clear queue of learners going silent so I message the right people before they withdraw.

Secondary readers: a platform owner comparing course health across modules, and an analyst who must defend the early-window definition against the Open University learning-analytics tradition in that same review.

images.alt: BryTek instructor dashboard from the UX case study
images.caption: UX companion: the cockpit this retention story is meant to feed.

sections.title: Scenario: who needs outreach
sections.body:
DATA QUESTION - In the first two weeks of a presentation, which enrollments should enter an instructor intervention queue because early VLE silence predicts Fail or Withdrawn?

That question is not arbitrary. Open University research on at-risk detection treats early engagement with the Virtual Learning Environment as a practical signal for tutor and support action. This portfolio study asks the same question in BryTek language: who should the teacher message now?

Source choice follows that context. Thin Hugging Face engagement samples and AI-tutor grade logs do not match an instructor cockpit. The Open University Learning Analytics Dataset does: real module presentations, final results, and daily VLE click summaries published for learning-analytics research.

The storyboard: define the retention outcome, measure early clicks, rank risk bands, read module health, then leave with a decision mix a teacher can own.

sections.title: Process: define the risk outcome
sections.body:
Grain is student x module presentation (enrollment). Final result is the outcome label.

INSIGHT - Pass and Distinction are success. Fail and Withdrawn are the at-risk bucket for intervention planning (53% of enrollments in this extract).

That binary matches the human job. Before finer grading models, a teacher needs to know who is drifting out of the course entirely.

images.alt: Open University Learning Analytics Dataset final outcome counts
images.caption: Outcomes: Pass 12,361 · Withdrawn 10,156 · Fail 7,052 · Distinction 3,024.

sections.title: Process: early VLE engagement
sections.body:
Early window: days 0 to 14 on the Open University Learning Analytics Dataset relative course calendar. Sum of VLE clicks per enrollment. Missing VLE rows count as zero early clicks. The window is short on purpose: retention action is useful in the first weeks, not after the final result lands.

INSIGHT - Risk falls as early activity rises: 86% at-risk in the lowest quintile vs 28% in the highest. Silent starters (14% of enrollments) hit 91% Fail or Withdrawn.

That pattern is why BryTek's recent-activity and course-health widgets should amplify early silence, not only lifetime averages.

images.alt: At-risk rate by early VLE engagement quintile
images.caption: Q1 86% at-risk vs Q5 28% (first 14 days).

images.alt: At-risk rate for silent starters versus learners with early activity
images.caption: Zero early clicks: 91% at-risk (14% of enrollments).

sections.title: Process: course health by module
sections.body:
Teachers do not only triage people. They also need to know which courses are structurally harder.

INSIGHT - Module CCC shows the highest at-risk rate in this cut (62%, withdraw 45%, 4,434 enrollments). Module AAA leads success at 71%.

On BryTek, that belongs next to My Courses: a health stripe, not only a title list.

images.alt: Pass and withdrawal rates by Open University Learning Analytics Dataset module
images.caption: Module health: success vs withdrawal side by side.

sections.title: Decision storyboard
sections.body:
DECISION - Week-2 outreach queue: message every silent starter first (14% of enrollments, 91% at-risk), then prioritize the lowest early-engagement quintile (86% at-risk). Staffing review: inspect module CCC for content or support gaps before the next presentation.

INSIGHT - The BryTek dashboard earns its KPI row when those queues exist. Enrollments and earnings without an intervention list leave the human retention problem unsolved.

DATA QUESTION - After two weeks of outreach, did silent starters return to the VLE, and did Fail + Withdrawn fall in the next assessment window?

images.alt: Silent starter risk for the intervention decision
images.caption: Decision anchor: silent starters enter the queue first.

sections.title: What we learned
sections.body:
A client justification needs a reason before it needs a chart. Here the reason is triple: protect online learners who go quiet, sit that job inside a published Open University learning-analytics tradition, and deliver it beside BryTek UX so design and data argue for the same build decision.

Say the source out loud. Claiming the Open University Learning Analytics Dataset as BryTek production data would break trust with the client.

Early windows beat lifetime averages for instructor action. Teachers intervene in weeks, not after the final result lands.

Keep the pair visible in reviews. Design shows the cockpit; analytics shows why silent starters belong on the first scroll.

sections.title: Future
sections.body: Next depth on the same human and scientific question: first-assessment timing, weekly engagement trajectories, and a lightweight risk score behind BryTek's recent-activity panel. Keep Retail KPI Storytelling as the separate commerce data study.

projects.title: Friends of Figma (FoF) Tunisia
projects.impact: 290 members on the official FoF network; hosted Tunisia Config Watch Party 2026 and recurring meetups; speaking at DevFest, GDG MENAT Summit, and Women Techmakers.
projects.problem: Tunisia lacked an official Figma-affiliated community space for designers and builders to learn together in public.
projects.summary: I founded and lead Friends of Figma (FoF) Tunisia, listed on the official Friends of Figma network as Tunisia’s chapter for Africa. As Figma Community Advocate and organizer, I grow a local space for designers and builders through meetups, Config watch parties, speaker programs, and learning in public - the same communication craft I bring to data work.

aboutPage.bio: I'm Amani, a Data Analyst in Ariana, Tunisia. I analyze complex datasets and build KPI narratives with SQL and Python, with a designer's eye for clarity.
aboutPage.title: About me in 2 minutes

vision.body: I have a clear plan for growth in the coming years: deepen analytical craft while continuing to lead with design clarity. I want to shape narratives from data and from product experience, make sharper decisions, and drive design thinking across teams. I aspire to lead, foster learning in public, and build work that people trust and enjoy using.
vision.title: Vision

aboutPage.bioDesign: I'm Amani, a UX-minded designer and Friends of Figma Tunisia advocate. I shape brand storytelling, mobile product flows, and research-led interfaces across freelance client work.

experience.title: E-Powertrain Software Tester
bullets: Analyzed and validated large-scale Hardware-in-the-Loop (HiL) test datasets for ECUs, BMS, and Relay Boxes, identifying performance trends and system anomalies.
Automated data collection and defect-tracking workflows in JIRA, improving data traceability and cutting analysis turnaround time.
Extracted, cleaned, and interpreted calibration and diagnostic data using INCA to support parameter optimization and reporting.

experience.title: Automotive Software Tester
bullets: Built Python-based automation scripts to generate and analyze test-result data for E/E vehicle systems (BMW Gen6, G2X platforms), improving data accuracy and reporting reliability.
Consolidated and analyzed validation datasets spanning BMS, wiring, ECUs, and CAN networks, converting raw results into clear, actionable reports for engineering teams.
Tracked and reported defect and test-coverage metrics in JIRA, supporting data-driven decisions across hardware, software, and systems teams.

experience.title: Product Owner
bullets: Analyzed product requirements and team input to define structured, data-informed functional specifications.
Prioritized product backlog using data on technical risk, system dependencies, and delivery milestones.
Coordinated cross-functional teams to align data-related deliverables with business objectives.

workProcess.body: Understand business goals and project requirements with clients, then run user research including interviews, surveys, analytics, and competitor analysis. Identify pain points and motivations through persona creation.
workProcess.title: Research & discovery

workProcess.body: Map content and navigation with sitemaps and user flows. Prioritize features that align with user needs and product goals so the structure stays usable and purposeful.
workProcess.title: Information architecture & planning

workProcess.body: Craft low-fidelity wireframes to shape layout and functionality, then bring designs to life with interactive prototypes for testing and validation.
workProcess.title: Wireframing & prototyping

workProcess.body: Enhance the interface with branding, typography, and color while keeping accessibility and consistency. Add micro-interactions when they improve clarity and polish.
workProcess.title: UI design & visual development

workProcess.body: Conduct usability testing, gather feedback, and refine wireframes, prototypes, and visuals based on real user interactions.
workProcess.title: Testing & iteration

workProcess.body: Prepare clear handoff for engineering so the validated experience can be built with consistent components, states, and flows.
workProcess.title: Handoff & implementation

workProcess.body: Track behavior with analytics and heatmaps, gather feedback, and keep improving engagement through continuous design updates.
